section.title: The Voice of the Lord
section.sectionRationale: A hymn of David that pictures a great storm rolling in from the sea and sweeping across the land as the very voice of the LORD, calling heavenly beings to ascribe glory to Him, and closing with the LORD enthroned as King forever, who gives His people strength and peace.
section.thesis: The sevenfold voice of the LORD, thundering with power over the cedars and the wilderness alike, proclaims that the same God enthroned over the flood as King forever is the one who gives strength and peace to His own people.
section.frameNote: Three movements: a call for heavenly beings to ascribe glory and strength to the LORD (1-2); the sevenfold voice of the LORD thundering through the storm, climaxing in the cry of 'Glory' in His temple (3-9); and the LORD enthroned as King forever, giving strength and peace to His people (10-11).

units.label: Ascribe to the LORD glory and strength
units.note: a call to the heavenly beings

verses.text: Ascribe to the LORD, O heavenly beings, ascribe to the LORD {{kavod|glory}} and strength.
Ascribe to the LORD the glory due His name; worship the LORD in the splendor of His holiness.

The {{qol|voice}} of the LORD is over the waters; the God of glory thunders; 
the LORD is heard over many waters.
The voice of the LORD is powerful; the voice of the LORD is majestic.
The voice of the LORD breaks the cedars; the LORD shatters the cedars of Lebanon.
He makes Lebanon skip like a calf, and Sirion like a young wild ox.
The voice of the LORD strikes with flames of fire.
The voice of the LORD shakes the wilderness; the LORD shakes the Wilderness of Kadesh.

The voice of the LORD twists the oaks and strips the forests bare. 
And in His temple all cry, "Glory!"

units.label: The LORD is enthroned as King forever
units.note: strength and peace for His people

verses.text: The LORD sits enthroned over the flood; the LORD is enthroned as King forever.
The LORD gives His people strength; the LORD blesses His people with {{shalom|peace}}.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weight, honor, the visible weightiness of God's presence and character. Heavenly beings are called to ascribe, to acknowledge in words, the glory that already and fully belongs to God.

qol.gloss: Voice, sound. Repeated seven times in this psalm, the voice of the LORD is inseparable from the LORD Himself, His speech accomplishing exactly what it declares, a pattern that culminates in the eternal Word who was with God and was God, John 1:1.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being, more than the absence of conflict. The same voice that shatters cedars and shakes the wilderness is the voice that blesses God's people with this full, settled peace.

basic.context: David calls the heavenly beings to ascribe to the LORD the glory and strength due His name. A great storm then rises over the Mediterranean Sea and sweeps east over the land, and the psalm pictures this storm as the voice of the LORD, thundering, breaking cedars, shaking the wilderness, until in God's temple all creation cries out, 'Glory!' The psalm closes with the LORD enthroned over the flood as King forever, who gives His people strength and blesses them with peace.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm should be read as worship shaped by the natural world's testimony to God's power, not nature worship itself; the storm does not compete with the LORD but is simply His voice, and the psalm's real center is not meteorology but praise.
basic.originalLanguages: The heavenly beings are called to ascribe kavod, glory, weighty honor, to the LORD. His qol, voice, is repeated seven times, inseparable from His own person and power, and the psalm closes with the LORD giving His people shalom, full and settled peace, not merely calm weather but wholeness of life.
basic.typology: The voice of the LORD that thunders with creative and destructive power anticipates the eternal Word, John 1:1, through whom all things were made, and the same voice that stills or stirs the sea anticipates Jesus, who speaks and even wind and sea obey Him, Mark 4:39-41.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Word, the voice of the LORD made flesh, John 1:1, 14, through whom all things were created and by whose word the disciples watched wind and sea obey instantly, Mark 4:39-41, provoking the same question this psalm answers, 'Who then is this?' He is also the King enthroned forever, fulfilling verse 10's vision of the LORD reigning over the flood, and the giver of the true peace this psalm closes with, not as the world gives, John 14:27.
basic.god: God's voice is shown as inseparable from His own power and presence, capable of both overwhelming, world-shaking force and tender blessing, giving strength and peace to the very people who might otherwise tremble at His majesty.
basic.crossReferences: John 1:1-3 identifies Jesus as the eternal Word through whom all things were made, echoing this psalm's voice that shapes creation. Mark 4:39-41 shows Jesus commanding wind and sea with a word, provoking wonder at who He must be. John 14:27 shows Jesus giving His own peace, not as the world gives, fulfilling the psalm's closing blessing.
basic.oneStory: The sevenfold voice of the LORD that thunders through the storm and shakes creation, while all in His temple cry 'Glory,' finds its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the eternal Word through whom all things were made, who commands wind and sea, reigns as King forever, and gives His people true and lasting peace.

context.literary: A hymn of David built around a sevenfold repetition of 'the voice of the LORD,' tracing a storm's path from the Mediterranean Sea across Lebanon and south to the Wilderness of Kadesh, framed by a call to worship at the start and a blessing of strength and peace at the close.
context.historical: Likely composed against the backdrop of ancient Near Eastern storm imagery, in which surrounding nations attributed thunder and lightning to rival storm deities; David instead credits every element of the storm to the LORD alone, no rival power sharing His glory.
context.canonical: Belongs to the wider biblical pattern of theophany, God's powerful appearing, seen also at Sinai, Exodus 19, and echoed in the New Testament's presentation of Jesus commanding creation itself, Mark 4:39-41, and of the Word through whom all things were made, John 1:1-3.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call God's people, and even the heavenly beings, to ascribe glory to the LORD alone by tracing His unrivaled power through the natural world, and to ground confident trust in the fact that this same powerful God gives His people strength and peace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific storm as a vehicle for praise; it functions prescriptively as an invitation for every generation to see God's power displayed in creation and respond with worship rather than fear alone.

debates.question: Does Psalm 29's language and structure suggest it was adapted from an earlier Canaanite hymn to the storm god Baal, repurposed to praise the LORD instead?

views.label: Some scholars note similarities between this psalm's poetic structure and imagery and Ugaritic hymns to Baal, suggesting Israelite worship deliberately took up familiar storm-god language and redirected it entirely to the LORD, asserting His supremacy over any rival claim
views.case: The psalm's focus on thunder, cedars of Lebanon, and the language of enthronement over the flood parallels known ancient Near Eastern storm deity hymns, and such deliberate redirection would itself be a bold theological statement, that the true storm-god is the LORD alone.

views.label: The psalm should be read simply as an Israelite composition describing God's power over creation using imagery natural to anyone living near the Mediterranean and Lebanon's cedar forests, without needing to posit direct literary dependence on pagan sources
views.case: Storm imagery for divine power is a natural human response to violent weather across many cultures, and the psalm's content is thoroughly monotheistic and consistent with the rest of the Psalter's celebration of the LORD's sole sovereignty over nature, without requiring a specific borrowed source.

debates.bearing: Either reading arrives at the same theological point: whatever imagery or background the psalm draws upon, its message is an uncompromising claim that the LORD alone, not any rival storm deity, commands the wind, the thunder, and the whole created order.

study.originalLanguages: The heavenly beings are called to ascribe kavod, glory, to the LORD, whose qol, voice, is repeated seven times as inseparable from His own power, and who closes the psalm by giving His people shalom, full and settled peace, not merely the absence of storm.

typology.type: The voice of the LORD shaping and shaking creation, verses 3-9
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the eternal Word through whom all things were made, John 1:1-3, who commands wind and sea with a word, Mark 4:39-41.

typology.type: The LORD enthroned as King forever, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: Christ's eternal reign as King, and His gift of true peace to His people, John 14:27.

study.christ: Jesus is the Word, the voice of the LORD made flesh, John 1:1, 14, through whom all things were created. When He commands wind and sea and they instantly obey, Mark 4:39-41, the disciples' astonished question, 'Who then is this?' is answered by this very psalm: the LORD whose voice breaks cedars and shakes the wilderness. He is the King enthroned forever, verse 10, and the giver of the true peace with which this psalm closes, a peace not as the world gives, John 14:27.
study.god: God's voice and God's person are inseparable, powerful enough to shatter cedars and shake the wilderness, yet the same voice that overwhelms creation is the voice that gives strength and blesses His own people with peace.

crossReferences.note: Jesus identified as the eternal Word through whom all things were made, echoing this psalm's creative, world-shaping voice.
Jesus commands wind and sea with a word, prompting the same wonder this psalm's storm theophany evokes.
Jesus gives His own peace, not as the world gives, fulfilling the psalm's closing blessing of strength and peace.

study.oneStory: The sevenfold voice of the LORD, thundering through the storm and shaking all creation while heaven cries 'Glory,' finds its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the eternal Word through whom all things were made, who commands creation itself, reigns as King forever, and gives His people lasting peace.

quiz.q: Who is called to ascribe glory and strength to the LORD in verse 1?

options.note: The psalm opens by summoning the heavenly beings, not merely humans, to worship.

options.text: Only the kings of the earth.
options.note: The call is specifically to heavenly beings, not earthly kings, in this opening verse.

options.text: All the people of Israel gathered at worship.
options.note: Close, since Israel does ascribe glory to God elsewhere, but verse 1 specifically addresses heavenly beings, angelic beings, not the gathered human congregation.

options.text: No one; it is a private meditation.
options.note: The psalm opens with a direct, public call to worship, not private meditation.

quiz.q: How many times is the phrase 'the voice of the LORD' repeated in verses 3-9?

options.note: The sevenfold repetition structures the psalm's central section and its picture of the storm.
The repetition occurs seven times across these verses, more than three.
Close, an undercount by two, but the deliberate refrain 'the voice of the LORD' actually appears seven times across verses 3-9.
The correct count within this section is seven, not twelve.

quiz.q: What happens 'in His temple' according to verse 9?

options.note: This is the climactic response to the LORD's voice, worship breaking out in His temple.

options.text: All bow down in reverent silence, without any spoken response.
options.note: Close, since reverence is present, but the verse specifically describes a spoken, exultant cry, 'Glory,' not silent reverence.
The text specifies a cry of worship, 'Glory,' not a description of sacrificial ritual.
The verse describes a loud cry of worship, the opposite of silence.

quiz.q: What is the LORD said to be enthroned over in verse 10?

options.note: The verse pictures the LORD enthroned over the flood, and as King forever.

options.text: The nations of the earth only.
options.note: While the LORD reigns over the nations elsewhere in Scripture, this verse specifically names the flood.

options.text: The waters of the sea generally, without reference to a specific flood.
options.note: Close, since water imagery runs through the psalm, but verse 10 uses a specific term, the flood, echoing the great flood of Genesis, not water in general.
The verse explicitly says the LORD is enthroned, actively reigning, not hidden.

quiz.q: What two things does the LORD give His people in verse 11?

options.note: The psalm closes with this twofold blessing, strength and peace, for God's people.

options.text: Comfort and rest, without specific mention of strength.
options.note: Close in feeling, since comfort and rest are related to peace, but verse 11 specifically names strength alongside peace as the twofold gift.
This is the opposite of the blessing described, which is strength and peace.
The closing verse names strength and peace specifically, not material possessions.

questions: The psalm calls even heavenly beings to ascribe glory to the LORD. What in creation, a storm, the ocean, the night sky, most often moves you to worship rather than mere observation?

The voice of the LORD is powerful enough to shatter cedars and shake the wilderness, yet it is the same voice that blesses His people with peace. How do you hold together God's overwhelming power and His tender care in your own picture of Him?
In the temple, all creation's upheaval resolves into a single cry, 'Glory.' What in your life currently feels like upheaval, and how might worship reframe it rather than only fear or complaint?
The psalm insists the LORD alone, not any rival power, commands the storm. What competing sources of security or control are you tempted to trust instead of God's sovereign voice?
The psalm closes with strength and peace as gifts from God to His people. Where do you most need God's strength or peace this week, and how will you ask Him for it specifically?

translationNotes.note: Heavenly beings translates a Hebrew phrase literally meaning sons of God or sons of might, understood as angelic beings called to worship.
Sirion is another name for Mount Hermon, paired with Lebanon to describe the storm's path skipping across the region's great mountains like young animals.

Some manuscripts and translations render this line 'makes the oaks to shake' rather than describing deer giving birth, a matter of vowel pointing in the Hebrew consonantal text; either reading conveys the same overwhelming power of God's voice.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 29:1-11. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was found specifically expositing Psalm 29 in searches conducted this session, so sermon status is none.
